name: CoFo Chimera
classification_notes: CoFo Chimera is a reversed-contrast display typeface influenced by calligraphy at a steep 45° angle, with high-contrast statement styles and lower-contrast text companions. Reversed contrast places it firmly in display territory.
summary: A reversed-contrast display family by Maria Doreuli that reframes the genre's intentional ugliness as something calligraphic and elegant.
description: Where historical reversed-contrast typefaces courted deliberate strangeness, CoFo Chimera draws on a broad-nib calligraphic angle of 45° to produce letterforms that feel characterful rather than awkward. The family spans five styles: three high-contrast weights for display use and two lower-contrast companions suited to smaller sizes, each drawn from scratch rather than interpolated. Latin and Cyrillic coverage makes it practical across a range of editorial and identity contexts.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Chimera as an experiment in bringing dynamism and beauty to traditionally ugly reversed-contrast type. Designed by Maria Doreuli and released in 2018, the family draws on calligraphy made with a broad-nib tool at a steep 45° angle, transforming the genre's inherent quirkiness into something friendlier. It comprises three high-contrast display styles and two low-contrast companions, all sharing the same concept but each drawn entirely from scratch.

name: CoFo Drifter
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes CoFo Drifter as a monospaced typeface, designed to address the classic spacing challenges inherent to monospaced type through slanted verticals.
summary: A monospaced typeface by Egor Golovyrin that counters the cold rigidity typical of the genre by slanting single-stroke verticals to create active, eye-catching diagonals.
description: Designed to solve a longstanding problem in monospaced type, CoFo Drifter tilts narrow verticals such as i and l to fill white space and add visual energy without sacrificing legibility. Open letterforms and vertical stroke cuts give it a friendly, approachable quality despite its technical structure. Available in seven weights from Hairline to Black with full variable font support, it covers Latin and Cyrillic and suits both branding and body typography.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Drifter as a monospaced typeface that escapes digital coldness, with a charismatic personality that is not alien to self-discipline and a love for technical work. To address the large white spaces around single-stroke letters like i, l, т and г, the designers slanted those verticals, creating provocative active diagonals that catch the eye. Open shapes and vertical stroke cuts give the typeface a sociable, friendly character, making it suitable for both a leading role in branding and detailed typographic composition alongside other typefaces.

name: CoFo Cinema1909
classification_notes: A caps-only display typeface drawing on both Latin Art Nouveau and Cyrillic Art Deco traditions, with compact serifs, strong thick-thin stroke contrast and dynamic proportions optimised for headline setting.
summary: A caps-only display serif by Liza Rasskazova, fusing Latin Art Nouveau and Cyrillic Art Deco influences into a tightly spaced headline face with deliberately dynamic proportions.
description: Designed by Liza Rasskazova and released in 2020, this single-style typeface works exclusively in capitals, with tightly spaced, carefully kerned letterforms built for dense headline texture. The proportions sit between narrow and wide, creating a strong rhythmic pattern reinforced by compact serifs, minimal extending elements and a considered balance of thick and thin strokes. Its dual historical inheritance, drawing on both Latin Art Nouveau freedom and an underdeveloped strain of Cyrillic Art Deco, gives it a character that sits outside any straightforward historical period.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe Cinema1909 as a typeface that resists easy classification, drawing simultaneously from Latin Art Nouveau and Cyrillic Art Deco to create what they call their own cinematic universe. It is caps only and optimised for headlines, with tight spacing, compact extending elements for minimal line spacing, and proportions described as intentionally dynamic rather than narrow or wide. They position it as a visual experiment that questions historical norms and the reader's aesthetic sensitivity.

name: CoFo Glassier
classification_notes: A narrow display face with subtle stroke contrast drawing on mid-century Soviet typography and American architectural lettering, placing it firmly in the display serif category.
summary: A narrow display serif by Liza Rasskazova drawing on mid-1950s to mid-1960s Soviet typography and American architectural lettering traditions.
description: Designed by Liza Rasskazova and released in 2023, CoFo Glassier pulls from two distinct sources: the graphic typography of the Soviet modernist period and lettering found on American buildings. Its narrow proportions and restrained stroke contrast allow it to function at medium text sizes as well as large display settings. Originally commissioned for Peredelkino, it covers both Latin and Cyrillic scripts in a single Regular style.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Glassier as an elegant display typeface inspired by modernism, combining influences from USSR typography of the mid-1950s to mid-1960s with references to American architectural lettering. They note its narrow proportions and subtle stroke contrast make it suitable for both large and medium-sized text, and that its refined character lends itself as a distinctive accent in design projects.

name: CoFo Gothic
classification_notes: CoFo Gothic draws directly from 19th and early 20th century grotesque advertising types, with narrow proportions, tight spacing and increased contrast typical of that tradition, updated with contemporary detailing.
summary: A confident grotesque drawing on 19th-century advertising types, designed by Maria Doreuli with narrow proportions, tight spacing and asymmetrical details that push against neo-grotesque neutrality.
description: Built on the crispness and narrow proportions of early gothic advertising faces, this typeface deliberately reintroduces warmth through asymmetric details and large lowercase letters rather than ironing them out. Twelve styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, and a variable font is available alongside the static cuts. Suited to branding, editorial and packaging where a sans needs character without tipping into decoration.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Gothic as a daring typeface that resists the lifelessness of neutrality, drawing on the best features of early grotesques used in 19th and early 20th-century advertising: crispness, narrow proportions, tight spacing, increased contrast and large lowercase letters. These are integrated into modern forms that retain the heartwarming irregularities distinguishing Gothics from dry neo-grotesques, with asymmetrical details lending an unusual friendliness across uses from branding and advertising to editorial and packaging.

name: CoFo FlicFlac
classification_notes: FlicFlac is a single-weight display typeface with an organic, modular-like construction built from water-drop forms that deliberately push legibility, placing it firmly in the display category with an expressive, decorative character.
summary: A single-weight display typeface by Maria Doreuli built from water-drop forms that create an illusion of modular repetition while almost no element is truly repeated.
description: Designed by Maria Doreuli and released in 2020, FlicFlac constructs its letterforms from soft, rounded elements that tighten and meet at the centre without touching, mimicking the natural flow of water. Despite the harmonious rhythm that words produce, each character is individually balanced rather than assembled from shared building blocks. The typeface covers Latin and Cyrillic, works at large scale for statement use, generates ornamental text textures at smaller sizes, and its component forms are well suited to motion and animation work.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe FlicFlac as an organic, soft and rhythmic typeface that explores repetition outside the strict rules of traditional stencil and modular design. They note that its water-drop-like main element flows naturally, with forms tightening in the middle of each letterform without touching, and that in more complex characters these drops merge into new shapes. The foundry acknowledge that the typeface pushes the limits of legibility, framing that as an intentional trade-off for a design that always demands attention.

name: CoFo Holz
classification_notes: Combines wedge-shaped triangular serifs with circular dots and terminals, drawing on 18th-century engravings and American wood type traditions; the modular geometric construction places it firmly in the display serif category.
summary: A variable display serif drawing on 18th-century engraving and American wood type, contrasting triangular wedge serifs with circular terminals across seven weights.
description: Designed by Nikita Sapozhkov and released in 2024, CoFo Holz pairs sharply pointed wedge serifs with soft circular dots and terminals, producing a deliberate geometric tension rooted in wood-type tradition. The name references the German word for wood, acknowledging the historical letterforms carved from wooden blocks that inform its exaggerated serifs. Seven weights from Thin to Black are available as static styles or as a single variable font, with coverage for Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Holz as an expressive serif with unapologetic character, balancing sharpness and softness through triangular wedge serifs and circular terminals. Its inspiration comes from 18th-century engravings and American wood type, both noted for exaggerated pointed serifs, and the name Holz (German for wood) reflects that heritage. The foundry position it as a contemporary functional design that moves beyond its historical references, offering versatility from elegance to playfulness across its full weight range.

name: CoFo Kabeltouw
classification_notes: A modular, all-caps stencil typeface with an industrial and maritime character; classified as display due to its strong visual identity, reduced character set and stencil construction that limits continuous text use.
summary: A modular stencil typeface by Egor Golovyrin drawing on maritime industry aesthetics, available in four stencil grades plus a variable font axis controlling stencil openness.
description: Designed by Egor Golovyrin and released in 2023, CoFo Kabeltouw is built from a limited set of modules referencing shipping containers, cargo cranes, painted stencil lettering and metal pipework. Four static styles run from Solid 0 through Stencil 20, 38 and 56, with a variable font allowing continuous adjustment of stencilness to suit scale and context. The family covers Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, operates entirely in all-caps, and includes a generous range of alternates to compensate for the absence of lowercase.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Kabeltouw as a modular typeface drawing on modern maritime transportation — containers, cargo cranes, curved pipes, signal colours and stencil typography — combining strict mechanical logic with the warmth of hand-painted letters. The name derives from a nautical unit of length equal to one tenth of a nautical mile. The variable version allows designers to adjust stencilness to match scale and context, and the foundry position it as suited to branding, advertising and complex typographic systems.

name: CoFo Hand
classification_notes: A non-connecting script designed to evoke casual, everyday handwriting rather than formal calligraphy or decorative lettering, placing it firmly in the casual handwriting subcategory of script typefaces.
summary: A non-connecting casual handwriting script by Liza Rasskazova, covering Latin and Cyrillic, designed for legibility at small sizes without the formality of calligraphic scripts.
description: Designed by Liza Rasskazova with team support from Maria Doreuli, Elena Novoselova and Tasya Petelina, CoFo Hand sits between the spontaneity of personal handwriting and the usability demands of set text. Its disconnected letterforms avoid the awkward joins that often plague script typefaces, keeping it readable across a wide range of sizes and contexts. The single Regular style covers both Latin and Cyrillic, making it a practical choice for multilingual projects that need an informal, human quality.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Hand as a friendly script with the essence of lively handwriting, positioning it as neither a child's scribble nor a formal wedding script, but the casual kind used for grocery lists and phone-call notes. They highlight the non-connecting letters as a key feature that keeps the typeface easy to use across contexts and legible even at small sizes.

name: CoFo Raffine
classification_notes: Described as high-contrast with exaggerated, asymmetric proportions and swashes, designed explicitly for large sizes and expressive typesetting, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A high-contrast display typeface with deliberately exaggerated, asymmetric proportions, originally inspired by the title lettering of a 1919 silent film.
description: Designed by Olga Kovalenko and released in 2025, CoFo Raffine pairs dramatic variation in character widths with exaggerated individual letterforms and a carefully chosen set of swashes and ligatures. The result is a typeface that reads as deliberately unpredictable at display sizes, drawing on early cinema lettering while arriving at something with its own confident energy. It covers Latin and Cyrillic scripts across five weights from Thin to Bold, with a variable font included in the complete family. Recognised by Communication Arts, Tokyo TDC, Type Directors Club and The One Club for Creativity.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Raffine as an eclectic typeface bursting with confidence, energy and style, inspired by the title lettering of a 1919 classic silent film. They note that amplified variation in proportions makes typesetting any word or title unpredictable and exciting, with the deliberate asymmetry extended further by swashes and ligatures. They recommend using it at larger sizes and emphasise that it avoids simplification at every turn.

name: CoFo Kak
classification_notes: A sans-serif with organic, hand-influenced character and an unconventional weight structure that shifts from condensed to wide across just three styles, placing it in the humanist sans tradition despite its geometric compactness.
summary: A three-weight sans by Maria Doreuli that deliberately forgoes superfamily conventions in favour of three tightly considered styles spanning condensed to wide.
description: Designed by Maria Doreuli and released in 2020, CoFo Kak covers Latin and Cyrillic in just three styles: Regular, Medium and Black. The family's proportions shift markedly across weights, moving from a condensed upright to a wide Black, giving designers distinct tools to combine within a single typeface. A large x-height, compact ascenders and tight spacing produce a dense, rhythmic texture suited to branding and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Kak as a powerful sans with an unconventional family structure, deliberately limited to three styles so each can be designed without compromise. The style variation from condensed Regular to wide Black is intentional, creating opportunities for exciting combinations. Taking the cactus as its symbol, the typeface is positioned around strength and endurance, with a large x-height and tight spacing that delivers an impactful texture for branding and editorial use.

name: CoFo Peshka
classification_notes: CoFo Peshka is a geometric sans-serif with a variable contrast axis, spanning from zero-contrast to high-contrast strokes across eight contrast levels and five weights, placing it in an experimental geometric sans tradition.
summary: A 144-style variable sans covering Latin, Cyrillic and Hebrew, built around a contrast axis that runs from zero-stroke-contrast to full high-contrast across eight steps and six weights.
description: The family ships with 144 static styles plus a variable font, combining eight contrast values (0 through 1000) with six weights from Thin to Black and three slopes: upright, backslanted and slanted. That contrast axis is the defining structural idea, letting the typeface shift from a plain geometric skeleton to something approaching a high-contrast display face within a single family. Cyrillic, Latin and Hebrew are all covered, making it one of the few variable typefaces to span three distinct scripts simultaneously.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry present CoFo Peshka as a variable typeface with 144 styles, offering Cyrillic, Latin and Hebrew script support. The family is organised around a contrast axis with eight contrast levels alongside six weights and three slope variants, available as a complete family, an uprights-only bundle or individual styles.

name: CoFo Robert
classification_notes: CoFo Robert draws directly from the Clarendon tradition, named after Robert Beasley who originated the style, with bracketed slab serifs and strong stroke contrast typical of the 19th-century mechanical letter category.
summary: A Clarendon-derived slab serif with sharp curves and expressive italics, named after the style's originator Robert Beasley and released across seven weights.
description: Taking its name from Robert Beasley, who created the first Clarendon typefaces in the early 19th century, this family bridges the mechanical authority of Industrial Revolution display type with a contemporary sharpness in its curves. Fourteen styles run from Light to Black with matching italics, and each includes decorative numbers, arrows, hand glyphs, bullets and small caps. Latin and Cyrillic coverage makes it a practical choice for bilingual publishing and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Robert as a modern twist on a classic, named after the originator of the Clarendon style and inspired by chunky mechanical letters designed in the early 19th century for large display use. They note that the expressive spirit of the Industrial Revolution is mixed with new technology and a human touch, producing a rational multifunctional family with authoritative Romans and expressive Italics, packed with decorative numbers, arrows, hands, bullets and small caps across seven weights.

name: CoFo Robert Sans
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a utilitarian grotesque with humanist warmth, derived structurally from its serif counterpart CoFo Robert, with moderate stroke contrast and softened curves rather than strict geometric or rationalist construction.
summary: A humanised grotesque by Liza Rasskazova, derived from the serif CoFo Robert, with moderate contrast and softened curves across seven weights for Latin and Cyrillic.
description: Rather than stripping forms to a rational minimum, CoFo Robert Sans takes its structural cues from the serif CoFo Robert, carrying over proportional warmth and moderate stroke contrast into a sans-serif frame. The result sits between a neutral grotesque and a humanist sans, with small caps, superscript and subscript figures, and an extensive character set covering both Latin and Cyrillic. Available as a variable font alongside seven static weights from Light to Black.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Robert Sans as a utilitarian sans with a human touch, designed by Liza Rasskazova and released in 2026. They frame it as a challenge to the idea of a neutral grotesque, using CoFo Robert as its foundation to bring warmth through moderate contrast, balanced proportions and softened curves, without sacrificing functionality. They call it a friendly workhorse with an extensive character set in both Latin and Cyrillic.

name: CoFo Sona
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a geometric sans with humanist influence; the overall structure is rational and orderly in the geometric tradition, with proportional and detail-level warmth drawn from humanist design.
summary: A geometric sans by Liza Rasskazova that tempers its rational structure with humanist proportions and a subtle play of contrasts, available in four weights with Latin and Cyrillic coverage.
description: Released in 2023, CoFo Sona sits in the rational geometric sans tradition while drawing on humanist proportions and detailing to introduce energy and movement into an otherwise orderly framework. The family comes in four weights from Regular to Black, each available as a variable font, covering both Latin and Cyrillic scripts. A reduced character set keeps the scope focused, and the slightly reversed contrast adds a quiet visual tension without disrupting legibility.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry describe CoFo Sona as a geometric sans with unexpected twists, merging neutrality and vitality into one typeface. They position it as belonging to the rational modern geometric category whilst giving it a strong humanist influence, particularly in its proportions and details, which they say creates an energetic and dynamic feel. A subtle play with contrasts adds stability and liveliness in equal measure, with the family available in four weights across Latin and Cyrillic.

name: CoFo Weather
classification_notes: The page offers no explicit classification, but the specimen shows a clean, upright sans-serif with variable width and weight axes, consistent with a contemporary grotesque family. The breadth of widths from Condensed to Extended and the Latin/Cyrillic dual-script support point to a functional grotesque.
summary: A large variable grotesque spanning five widths and six weights, with full italic coverage and dual Latin and Cyrillic support across 60 static styles.
description: CoFo Weather is distributed as both a variable font and a 60-style static family, running from Condensed Hairline to Extended Black with matching italics throughout. Five width grades — Condensed, Narrow, Normal, Wide and Extended — give the family considerable range for editorial and branding work across different column widths and languages. Coverage includes Latin and Cyrillic character sets, though the page notes a reduced character set relative to full-coverage families.
foundry_blurb: Contrast Foundry present CoFo Weather as a variable typeface available in 60 styles across five widths and six weights, offered with both upright and italic cuts, supporting Latin and Cyrillic scripts. It is available as a complete family, an uprights-only bundle, a ten-style selection or individual styles.
